analysis:
- Algorithmic styles: Heuristic 1 uses a constructive greedy-insert (GSI) followed by an explicit local-search (swap) refinement. Heuristic 2 is a simple greedy that picks the exact highest marginal hypervolume one-by-one. Heuristic 3 is an approximate greedy that estimates marginal contributions via Monte‑Carlo sampling and vectorized dominance counting, with box‑volume fallbacks.
- Quality vs cost tradeoffs: 1 and 2 compute exact hypervolume repeatedly; this yields higher-quality decisions but huge overhead because pg.hypervolume objects and compute() are reconstructed and the full set HV is recomputed for every candidate or swap. 3 sacrifices exactness for speed: one costly sampling stage and then efficient bitwise/counting operations yield much lower per‑candidate cost and good scalability.
- Repeated work and avoidable overheads: all codes repeatedly recompute the same facts (full HV or dominated relationships) instead of caching or incrementally updating. Heuristic 2 also uses Python-level membership checks and nested loops that can be replaced by boolean masks. Heuristic 1’s local search naively evaluates all swaps (O(k·(n−k)) HVs per pass) causing many redundant evaluations.
- Good practices in 3: vectorized dominance matrix, blocking for memory, early stopping and deterministic RNG for reproducibility, and sensible fallbacks (box volume) are practical and robust engineering choices.
- Weak points in 3: sampling introduces approximation error and memory/time scales with M·N; parameter M must be tuned or adaptive. Heuristic 1/2: lack of screening/candidate pruning and no reuse of dominance information.
- Opportunities: combine strengths — use fast approximate screening (dominance counts / MC) to reduce candidate set, then apply exact greedy/local search on a small shortlist. Cache hypervolume values and per‑point contributions; do incremental updates on swaps; avoid recreating HV objects and move heavy operations into vectorized/numpy or C-backed libraries. Limit local search neighborhood (best-k candidates) and use early-exit criteria to constrain runtime.
experience: Use a two‑stage pipeline: fast approximate screening (dominance counts, Monte‑Carlo or box‑volume) to shortlist candidates, then exact hypervolume greedy + limited local search on that shortlist. Cache HV and contribution deltas, update incrementally, vectorize dominance tests, and avoid recreating heavy objects inside loops. Tune sample size adaptively and use early stopping to balance quality and time.
current_reflection: Use a two‑stage pipeline: fast approximate screening (dominance counts, Monte‑Carlo or box‑volume) to shortlist candidates, then exact hypervolume greedy + limited local search on that shortlist. Cache HV and contribution deltas, update incrementally, vectorize dominance tests, and avoid recreating heavy objects inside loops. Tune sample size adaptively and use early stopping to balance quality and time.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: two-stage pipeline, approximate screening, hypervolume greedy, incremental cache, vectorized dominance, adaptive sampling, early stopping
- Advice: Fast-screen candidates (dominance counts, Monte Carlo, box-volume), then run exact hypervolume greedy with limited local search on the shortlist; cache HV and contribution deltas and update them incrementally; vectorize dominance checks; adapt sample size and stop early when gains diminish.
- Avoid: Recomputing heavy objects inside loops, running full exact HV on the entire set, non-incremental caches, unvectorized dominance tests, and fixed oversized sampling budgets.
- Explanation: Screening narrows work, caching and incremental updates prevent redundant computation, vectorization speeds batch tests, and adaptive sampling balances quality vs. runtime.
